Authority
Working surface only. Canonical state lives in Markdown/JSON and changes solely after explicit acceptance in chat.

How do base delay, growth factor, retry count, cap, and jitter shape total retry wait?

Adjust the parameters to feel how exponential backoff shapes the total wait a client accumulates across retries. Nothing here changes files.

  • band = jitter range for one attempt
  • tick = deterministic delay (no jitter)
  • dashed mark = expected total
  • dashed + “capped” = delay cap reached

Parameters

100 ms
2×
5
10000 ms

JavaScript is off, so the sliders are disabled; the model shows its default state below. Ask in chat for any other parameter set — for example: “show base 500 ms, factor 3, full jitter”.

Derived consequence

Per-attempt delay, jitter range, and cumulative worst-case wait
AttemptDelayJitter rangeCumulative worst-case
1 100 ms 50–100 ms 100 ms
2 200 ms 100–200 ms 300 ms
3 400 ms 200–400 ms 700 ms
4 800 ms 400–800 ms 1500 ms
5 1600 ms 800–1600 ms 3100 ms

Worst-case total across 5 attempts: 3100 ms · expected ≈ 2325 ms

Exploring — adjust the model, then record a conclusion

Recording a conclusion flips the working status to candidate-ready and readies the export; it stays a candidate until you confirm it in chat.

Facts (sourced)

  • E1Retry storms amplify outages: synchronized clients retry in waves. — AWS Architecture Blog — Exponential Backoff and Jitter (Marc Brooker, 2015)
  • E1Full jitter lowers the expected per-attempt delay from 75% to 50% of the capped exponential delay — one third less than equal jitter — while preserving the same worst case. — AWS Architecture Blog — Exponential Backoff and Jitter (Marc Brooker, 2015)
  • E2Backoff without a cap eventually produces waits longer than any user will tolerate. — Google SRE Book ch. 22 — Addressing Cascading Failures

Declared simplifications

  • Simplificationassumes an ideal clock and zero network transfer time
  • Simplificationtreats jitter as a deterministic range band, not sampled randomness
  • Simplificationsingle client shown; aggregate-load effects are described, not simulated
Why jitter matters

Without jitter, clients that failed together retry together, re-synchronising into waves that hammer a recovering service. Jitter spreads each client's next attempt across a random window, so load smooths out. Full jitter (0 to the delay) spreads more aggressively than equal jitter (half the delay to the delay): expected per-attempt delay falls from 75% to 50% of the capped exponential delay — one third less — while the worst case stays unchanged.

Candidate export
